reasoning: "Butterfly" refers to a biological kind - a specific life stage (adult form) of certain insects in the order Lepidoptera. As a natural kind, it carries essential properties: something is a butterfly in all possible worlds where it exists as such. It provides its own identity condition based on biological criteria (species membership, developmental stage). Instances are intrinsic wholes (complete organisms with functional unity). Butterflies are independent entities that don't intrinsically depend on other individuals to exist, though they have biological dependencies on their environment.

"Red" is a quality or property that can be applied to various entities. It is not essential to any particular entity (e.g., an apple can be red, green, or yellow). Therefore, it is non-rigid. It does not carry an identity condition on its own (-I), nor does it supply its own identity condition (-O). It is non-unifying (-U) because being red does not make something an intrinsic whole. It is independent (-D) as the color red does not depend on other entities to exist.

"Food" is a term that refers to substances consumed to provide nutritional support for an organism. It is not an essential property of any particular entity, as something can be food in one context but not in another (e.g., a plant is not food to itself but can be food to an animal). Therefore, it is non-rigid. "Food" does not carry its own identity condition (-I) because it does not provide criteria for distinguishing individual instances. It does not supply its own identity condition (-O) since it is a mixin. Instances of food can be unified wholes or parts of wholes (-U), and food items are independent entities (-D) that do not intrinsically depend on other entities to exist.

"Animal" is a term that refers to a broad biological category encompassing all members of the kingdom Animalia. As a natural kind, it carries essential properties that define what it means to be an animal. It provides its own identity condition based on biological criteria (membership in the kingdom Animalia). Instances are intrinsic wholes (complete organisms with functional unity). Animals are independent entities that don't intrinsically depend on other individuals to exist, though they have biological dependencies on their environment.

"RedApple" is a term that combines a sortal (Apple) with a contingent quality (Red). An apple can be red at one time and turn green or yellow at another time while remaining the same apple. The property "being red" is not essential to being that particular apple - it's a temporary state. Therefore, RedApple is Anti-Rigid (~R). As a restriction of the sortal "Apple", it inherits the identity condition from Apple (+I), but does not supply its own identity condition (-O) since the identity comes from being an apple, not from being red. It maintains unity (+U) as apples are intrinsic wholes. It is independent (-D) as red apples don't intrinsically depend on other entities to exist.

"Group" refers to a collection of individuals that are considered as a single entity. The term "group" is not rigid because the same set of individuals can form different groups in different contexts or at different times. For example, a set of people can be a family group in one context and a work group in another. The property does not carry its own identity condition (-I) because the identity of a group is derived from its members. It does not supply its own identity condition (-O) since the identity comes from the members of the group. Groups are typically considered as unified wholes (+U) because they are treated as single entities. However, groups are dependent (+D) on their members to exist, as without members, there is no group.

"Country" refers to a political and geographical entity that is typically defined by borders, government, and population. The term is rigid because something is a country in all possible worlds where it exists as such. It carries an identity condition based on political and geographical criteria (+I), but does not supply its own unique identity condition (-O) since the identity comes from being a political entity with specific characteristics. Instances of countries are unified wholes (+U), and countries are independent entities that don't intrinsically depend on other countries to exist (-D), though they may have political or economic dependencies.

"AmountOfMatter" refers to a quantity of matter, which is a non-rigid property because something can be an amount of matter in some contexts but not in others (e.g., a lump of clay can be molded into different shapes, each being a different amount of matter). It does not carry an identity condition on its own (-I) because it does not define what makes something the same amount of matter over time. It does not supply its own identity condition (-O) since it is a measure rather than a definer of identity. Instances of amounts of matter are not necessarily unified wholes (-U) as they can be aggregated or divided. Amounts of matter are dependent (+D) on the matter they quantify and the context in which they are measured.

"PhysicalObject" refers to entities that have physical properties and occupy space. This term is rigid because something that is a physical object in one possible world is a physical object in all possible worlds where it exists. It carries an identity condition (+I) because physical objects have criteria for identity (e.g., spatiotemporal continuity). However, it does not supply its own unique identity condition (-O) as the specific identity criteria come from more specific sorts (e.g., rock, tree). Physical objects are intrinsic wholes (+U) and are independent entities (-D) that do not intrinsically depend on other entities to exist.

"GroupOfPeople" refers to a collection of individuals that are considered together as a single entity. The property is anti-rigid (~R) because the same individuals can form different groups or no group at all in different contexts. It does not carry its own identity condition (-I) since the identity of a group is derived from its members. It does not supply its own identity condition (-O) because the identity of a group is dependent on the identities of its members. It is anti-unity (~U) because a group is strictly an aggregate of its members. It is dependent (+D) because the existence of a group depends on the existence of its members.

"LivingBeing" refers to entities that are alive, possessing characteristics such as metabolism, growth, and reproduction. This term is rigid because being alive is an essential property for all instances of living beings in all possible worlds. It carries an identity condition (+I) because being a living being involves specific biological criteria. However, it does not supply its own unique identity condition (-O) as the identity is inherited from more specific biological kinds (e.g., plants, animals). Instances of living beings are intrinsic wholes (+U), and they are independent (-D) as they do not intrinsically depend on other entities to exist, though they have biological dependencies on their environment.

"SocialEntity" refers to entities that exist within a social context, such as organizations, groups, or institutions. The term is not rigid because an entity can exist without being a social entity in some contexts (e.g., a person can exist without being part of any social entity). It carries identity criteria inherited from its instances (e.g., a specific organization has its own identity), but it does not supply its own unique identity condition. Instances of social entities are typically unified wholes, and they often depend on the interactions and relationships within a social structure to exist.

"Location" refers to a spatial position or place. It is not essential for an entity to have a specific location, as entities can change locations or exist without a definite location. Therefore, "Location" is non-rigid (-R). It does not carry an identity condition on its own (-I), nor does it supply its own identity condition (-O). Locations are not intrinsic wholes but rather points or regions in space (-U). Locations are dependent (+D) as they are defined relative to other entities or spatial frameworks.

"Fruit" is a term that refers to a category of plant products that contain seeds. It is not essential to all instances in all possible worlds (e.g., a tomato can be a fruit in one context but a vegetable in another). Therefore, it is non-rigid. The term "fruit" does not carry an identity condition on its own; it is a category that can apply to many different kinds of plants. It does not supply its own identity condition. Instances of fruits are typically unified wholes, and fruits do not fundamentally depend on external entities for their existence (though they may depend on plants to grow).

"Vertebrate" refers to a biological category of animals characterized by the presence of a vertebral column or spine. This property is essential to all instances of vertebrates in all possible worlds where they exist as such, making it rigid (+R). The term carries an identity condition (+I) based on biological criteria (presence of a vertebral column), and it supplies its own identity condition (+O). Instances are intrinsic wholes (+U), and vertebrates are independent entities (-D) that don't intrinsically depend on other individuals to exist, though they have biological dependencies on their environment.

"Caterpillar" refers to the larval stage of butterflies and moths. It is a phase of a butterfly's life cycle, and an entity can be a caterpillar at one time and transform into a butterfly at another time. Therefore, it is anti-rigid (~R). As a phase, it inherits the identity condition from the sortal "Butterfly" (+I), but does not supply its own identity condition (-O). Caterpillars are intrinsic wholes (+U) and are independent entities (-D) that do not depend on other entities to exist.

"Person" is a fundamental sortal term that refers to human beings. It is rigid because being a person is an essential property of all instances in all possible worlds. It carries identity criteria based on biological and psychological continuity. It supplies its own identity condition, as the concept of a person is distinct and provides a unique identity criterion. Instances of persons are unified wholes, with intrinsic psychological and biological unity. Persons are independent entities that do not intrinsically depend on other individuals to exist, though they may depend on their environment for survival.

"LegalAgent" refers to an entity that acts on behalf of another in a legal context. This is a role that entities can play, but it is not essential to their existence. For example, a person can be a legal agent in one context but not in another. Therefore, it is anti-rigid (~R). The identity of a legal agent is derived from the entity playing the role, so it carries identity (+I) but does not supply its own identity condition (-O). Legal agents are unified entities (+U) because they act as single, coherent agents in legal contexts. However, they are dependent (+D) because their existence as legal agents relies on the legal framework and the entities they represent.

"Entity" is a very general term that refers to anything that exists, whether concrete or abstract. It is not rigid because not all instances of things that exist are necessarily entities in all possible worlds (e.g., a particular instance of a thought or idea might not exist in all possible worlds). It does not carry an identity condition because it is too broad a category to provide specific criteria for identity. It does not supply its own identity condition because it is a top-level category that encompasses all other categories. It is not unifying because entities can be anything from concrete objects to abstract concepts, and they do not necessarily form intrinsic wholes. It is independent because entities, by definition, exist on their own.

"Organization" refers to a structured social entity designed for a specific purpose. It is a role-like term because an entity can be an organization in some contexts but not in others (e.g., a group of people can form an organization, but the same group might not be an organization in another context). Therefore, it is anti-rigid (~R). It carries identity criteria (+I) but does not supply its own unique identity condition (-O) since the identity comes from the members and structure of the organization. Instances of organizations are unified wholes (+U), and organizations are dependent (+D) on their members and structure to exist.

"Agent" is a term that refers to entities capable of acting or performing actions. An entity can be an agent in some contexts but not in others (e.g., a person is always a person but may or may not be acting as an agent at a given time). This suggests anti-rigidity. The property carries identity criteria inherited from its instances (persons, organizations, software agents), but doesn't supply its own unique identity condition. Instances of agents are typically unified wholes, and agents don't fundamentally depend on external entities for their existence (though they may depend on something to act upon).

"Apple" refers to a specific kind of fruit, which is a biological sortal. As a sortal, it carries essential properties that define what it means to be an apple. It provides its own identity condition based on biological criteria (species membership). Instances are intrinsic wholes (complete organisms with functional unity). Apples are independent entities that don't intrinsically depend on other individuals to exist, though they have biological dependencies on their environment.
